design_philosophy.core_loop: Select appropriate weapon/gadget for stage constraints, navigate side-scrolling level under time pressure, defeat boss, acquire new vehicle to progress to next world
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Time limit pressure combined with resource management (ammunition conservation vs. combat necessity) and stage-specific tool requirements
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Stranded space adventurer using improvised weapons and gadgets to escape hostile alien worlds and reach his date
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Mandatory time limits on every stage that drain health when expired (punitive design philosophy); forced use of specific 'remote' gadgets (trampoline, helicopter) to complete stages, removing player choice in approach; lightweight comedic tone with punishment mechanics

difficulty.lives_system: Standard lives system; health drains when time limit expires
difficulty.checkpointing: Per-stage checkpointing (must complete entire stage within time limit or lose life)
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Limited agency; mandatory gadget use removes player choice in solving stage puzzles. Time limits are fixed and non-negotiable. Punch-only fallback available when ammunition depleted, but serves as last resort rather than viable strategy

progression.upgrade_system: Weapon and gadget variety ('remotes') with distinct ammunition/stock pools; player selects which tools to deploy per stage
progression.unlock_mechanism: Linear progression through three worlds; completion of each world grants new vehicle to access subsequent world. Vehicle acquisition integrated into narrative (comically failing vehicles trap player in new worlds)
replayability_hooks: Multiple weapon/gadget options create tactical variation in stage approach
Boss encounters with distinct patterns require learning
Cart-race levels provide different gameplay rhythm
Varied environmental settings (domed cities, alien hives, biomechanical facilities, temples, magma chambers)

world_building_techniques: Environmental variety signals progression through alien worlds (domed space colonies → hive structures → biomechanical facilities → ancient temples → magma chambers)
Vehicle acquisition tied to story beats (failed vehicles trap protagonist, driving narrative forward)
Comedic narrative framing (date stands him up on asteroid, final date is unexpected blue robot, protagonist pursues red robot instead)
End-game twist subverts expectations and provides comedic closure

boss_design.philosophy: Encounters described as 'particularly enormous or swift enemies'; suggest imposing scale and speed-based threat patterns

danger_signals: Hostile asteroid setting establishes environmental antagonism
Multiple alien world types (hive areas, biomechanical facilities) suggest alien civilization structures
Boss encounters framed as notable encounters rather than narrative agents
environmental_storytelling: Domed space colony cities indicate terraformed or inhabited worlds
Alien hive-type areas suggest organized alien societies
Biomechanical facilities indicate technological or hybrid lifeforms
Ancient temples with haunted quality suggest ruins or archaeological sites
Magma chambers establish elemental hazard environments
